author: @Berkana
text: Robert, if you do a YouTube search for "kerosene wick stove", you'll see that they all have these crowns of wicks that make smokey flames, but when you put the big can-shaped secondary burn structure on them, they burn with a blue flame. The "can" on top of these burners pre-heats air and introduces it into the burn zone for complete combustion. It turns out gradually feeding more and more pre-heated air into the burn zone is better than feeding in all the preheated air all at once. With this method, you can convert a smokey kerosine flame into a hot burning blue flame.

author: @steveblack728
text:
Hi Robert, as an ex combustion engineer , with your diesel heater if you put a diffuser ( like a static propellor) in the device you will make the flame spin and introduce more air , especially if you add additional air holes in the diffuser, this was the principal we used to use with the old diesel burners( obviously we also had forced air and pressurised oil nozzles) but the principal is the same, you may even get a roar 😂
Steve form Wigan

author: @heywoodjabozoff9383
text: the woodgas stove can be modified to an "everything nice stove" by replacing the holes in the bottom of the inside can to its side (at a level above the fuel), and shielding it so as to prevent air from getting into the inside can. The vapors on the inside are sucked through by the venturi effect. The use of a fan to the outer can will produce a nearly blue flame.

author: @davidpritchett855
text: So this is more similar in function to the catalytic converter in a cars exhaust system.  Secondary air holes make for a smoother burn and the air is pulled in via the venturi effect. Compared to a pyrolysis stove which is more of a chimney where the air is pulled up by heat stratification.

So Cool and intersting
Tomorrow I will try one gassifier stove over the other. 
Cooking outdoor  in two different setups .   I am about to test  ground wallnut leaves ,  dried orange peels and dried blackberry branches  alongside wood (i know this works pretty well) . ... so essentialy kitchen and garden waste. 
First setup: A Dutch oven hung in a chamotte chimney with the stoves under it... (I normally use wood and one pyrolysis stove ... I'm interested if the burn will be cleaner )

Done it... stacking them inside the chimney did work pretty well. 
Orange peels and blackberry branches worked very well too.
The setup with the pyrolysis stoves in the burning chamber of my outdoor oven didn't work in the setup I used . . (The chamber was to small to stack them and using multible in a small burning chamber (I used 3 smalln ones ) proveed disatvantageous too. 
Next try will be a single broader one.
The grounded leaves do need another setup too. 
Building 3 small pyrolisis stoves,with  2 nails and a hammer, took me 30 minutes.
And in the end I had a pretty good goulash. :)

author: @C-M-E
text:
This isn't so much for you, Robert, but general principle for anyone who's interested. I noted a few changes a guy needed for a true smokeless outdoor firepit on another video on youtube some time ago, which was suffering from the same issues. The trick with combustion and efficiency is getting Complete combustion, which most examples don't (engines in particular are absolutely terrible as most folks know, maybe not the exact percentage at best case 28-34% efficient, but can figure out that there's a ton of heat being lost and unutilized).
For the firepit guy, I outlined how he could fabricate what was essentially a combustion liner for a jet engine with primary, secondary and tertiary air inlet holes; primary are a certain size for the diameter of the burn vessel/ring, more or less reducing by half in hole size around the circumference of the ring at each stage but increasing in number from secondary and tertiary.
It is it's own black art if you'll excuse the pun to combustion design, as even a millimeter or two difference in error will result in a overly saturated (rich) air/fuel ratio or even not being able to sustain combustion at all. 
He was quite pleased until I also informed him that now, due to it burning exponentially hotter, he would also need a heat shield as the material he'd chosen was bound to melt! Ceramics are a wonderful thing. 😉

replies.author: @wolfmanmike47
replies.text: I wonder if I could drill holes in my crock pot because it would work really good to make a small portable stove it is some sort of ceramic

replies.author: @C-M-E
replies.text: ​@wolfmanmike47 Youtube is terrible for letting you know there's been replies. Just happen to check this and saw your question. Anyway, crock pots are a version of stone ware, essentially clay with a bit of mica or quartz. It can handle quite a lot of heat but as with any stoneware product, the threshold for cracking is very thin. You can machine them to some degree, but you need diamond tipped or edged tools (easily found on masonry bits). At least be prepared for it to crack if you attempt it however. On the plus side, you can find them all day at thrift stores and the like really cheap if you want to get one to experiment with.

author: @francp3147
text:
Heavier hydrocarbons need more oxygen for proper combustion. Modern wood stoves have a 2ndary combustion chamber, where the heat is higher and results in a clean & soot-free burn.
Ultimately we do want a fuel with max Energy/Volume, at lowest price and without stink. I'd expect Ethanol to beat Methanol for stink-free heat. Retail Kerosene has more Joules/L but isn't cheap. White Gas fluid is great, but not cheap. Gasoline and Diesel have lots of J/L, but have smell smell issues.
A table or spider diagram is needed to summarize the key variables of E/L, E/$, Smell, Combustion. 🍻

author: @gerrys6265
text: Thanks for sharing this.  It does seem like a lot of refinements are required to get it to burn clean....the colour of the flame suggests to me that there is probably a lot of 'smoke' it is just not very visible. Shouldn't it be burning blue?

replies.author: @C-M-E
replies.text:
Flame color is more a result of what's being burned as fuel versus the efficiency of the burn itself via the inherent chemical and/or elemental properties. Hydrogen for instance burns optimally at ~5000* F and is bright red, whereas natural gas/methane burns around 2500-3000*F and is blue. Copper, when you get it to burn, is a brilliant shade of blue/green. 
A more practical example would be fireworks. 😉

author: @kentishbunyip6081
text: I had exactly the same problem as another viewer. I even tried a few drops of lighter fluid but as soon as that was exhausted, out went the flame. I re-listened and then heard Robert say not to coil it so tightly that it makes a plug. Aaaaaahhhhhhh! Tried again with a much looser coil of the carbon felt so that it only overlapped inside the 18mm diameter copper pipe by about 10mm.  Let it soak up, tried to light. Nothing! Added a few drops of lighter fluid and...... Taadaa! Flame was still alight some 10 minutes later.  In fact, I couldn't then put the flame out by blowing on it. The test site, my 6ft x 8ft greenhouse, was completely filled with smoke. Panic ensued, much laughter from husband. In my attempts to extinguish the flame, I then knocked it over. (Anyone know how to get expired Tesco sunflower oil residue off paving slabs?😐)  Anyway, have ordered a nice little brass flame "doffer" and will continue this project with the new modification for smoke free overwintering of plants, hopefully. Thanks Robert for your continued efforts.

author: @Jollyprez
text: Nothing new here - my 1940s era "Blue Flame" Magic Chef kerosene stove works same way - and the flame is adjustable.

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: I guess you missed the main point of these videos which is to show how you can make a heater from easily obtained materials for almost zero cost using cheap fuels that are most often just thrown away. In building one you also get a better understanding of how they work.  He is well aware that this is a well established technology.

replies.author: @Jollyprez
replies.text: @Milkybar3320011 Not needed so far - the original asbestos ones work fine. I will be testing some fiberglass replacements this winter.

author: @xmysef4920
text: Hey rob! I just have a question - when making your cans ”burn proof”, is it possible to do it in a way that doesn’t involve burning the plastic paint off? Thanks :)

replies.author: @mfbfreak
replies.text: Use fruit cans with a tin coating on the inside. Often 'fruit cocktail' and ananas cans have the tin coating, not the epoxy coating.

replies.author: @xmysef4920
replies.text: @mfbfreak Ah thanks! so I could just use pineapple/ananas cans?

replies.author: @xmysef4920
replies.text: @mfbfreak But wait, so those *don’t* have an epoxy coating and won’t smoke when I put heat onto it?

author: @SkepticalCaveman
text: If one lives near a forrest, the one can collect pinecones and burn them in a rocket stove. The rocket stove also burns sawdust cleanly.

replies.author: @kodfkdleepd2876
replies.text:
Probably 1000x more safe. Using these heavy fuels in a closed environment is extremely dangerous. There will be no way to design a simple device that will completely burn them and so there will always be some hydrocarbons and over time it will saturate the air which anyone breathing over long periods of time will end up with major health issues(essentially one is committing suicide by locking themself in their garage with their car running... but since it is so slow people are too dumb too realize what they are doing).

These should not be used in closed environments period. Too much risk. A fireplace may work but then why not just use wood? Better yet, just use several layers of clothing and learn to exercise. Men have been living without heat for 10's of thousands of years just fine. It's not that bad when managed. No one will freeze to death. People regularly work in -40C without dying. People just want to be "comfortable". Well... best solution is to stop letting financial terrorists destroy the world. The politicians, bankers, hedge funds, etc are all doing this. It's all about $$$.

author: @victoryfirst2878
text: What I am wondering about this unit is can  you smell the burning of diesel with the chimney can put on top ?? You got me think again Robert. Great minds think alike.

replies.author: @kodfkdleepd2876
replies.text: I'm sure you can. The smoke is only due to incomplete combustion due to lack of o2 and lack of a hot enough flame(so the larger hydrocarbons are not completely broken down). The problem is that it's not getting rid of 100% of the chemicals so there are going to be trace amounts(even CO which is dangerous). So when he shows these demonstrations they should not be actually used, if you used these things to "keep warm" and used them in your house you'll end up dead or with health issues. Of course with a better design they could be used but they are very dangerous for a number of reasons(one of which is accidentally knocking them over). The real solution would be to go hang all the psychopaths playing games with innocent peoples lives. There is plenty of fuel in the ground and the setup already exist to use it. It's just financial terrorists psychopaths playing their games that is creating all the problems. [It's 100% about $$$ and nothing else]

If these things are going to be used they should be done outside and used to heat up a boiler(which is much easier to do). Anyways, it's insane all around.
